concept: a recursive function that builds a list
cue: The base case returns a one-element list; each other call adds its own number in front of the smaller result.
note: Recursion solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller input and combining the answer. The base case (here n == 0) stops the calls; without one the recursion never ends.

snippet:
def subs(nums):
    if not nums:
        return [[]]
    first, rest = nums[0], nums[1:]
    without = subs(rest)
    with_first = [[first] + s for s in without]
    return without + with_first

subs([1, 2])
cue: For each element there are two branches: the results that leave it out, then those same results with it added in front.
note: Every subset problem is a series of yes/no choices: for each element you either include it or exclude it. Two choices per element gives 2 to the n subsets in total.

concept: counting the leaves of a binary choice tree
cue: Each element doubles the number of paths, and one empty list counts as a single path.
note: Each element splits every path into two, so the choice tree over n elements has 2 to the n leaves. This is why generating all subsets is O(n * 2^n) work.

prompt: Write `subsets(nums)` that returns every subset of the list `nums`, including the empty subset and the full list. The subsets may come out in any order. Assume the values are distinct. Use backtracking.
note: The choose / explore / un-choose pattern: append an element (choose), recurse to extend the current subset (explore), then pop it (un-choose) so the next branch starts clean. There are 2 to the n subsets and each takes O(n) to copy, so time is O(n * 2^n).
solution:
def subsets(nums):
    res = []
    path = []
    def backtrack(start):
        res.append(path[:])
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            path.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            path.pop()
    backtrack(0)
    return res
cue: Keep a running `path`; record a copy at every call, then try each later element in turn, adding it before the recursive call and removing it after.

concept: the choose / explore / un-choose pattern with a shared path
snippet:
def permute(nums):
    res = []
    path = []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(path) == len(nums):
            res.append(path[:])
            return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            used[i] = True
            path.append(nums[i])
            backtrack()
            path.pop()
            used[i] = False
    backtrack()
    return res

permute([1, 2])
cue: The first branch fixes the first number, walks all the way to a full arrangement, then unwinds and fixes the other number first.
note: One shared `path` is mutated in place. Each step chooses an unused element, explores by recursing, then un-chooses by undoing both the append and the used flag. Appending `path[:]` stores a copy so later changes do not corrupt saved answers.

prompt: Write `permute(nums)` that returns every ordering (permutation) of the list `nums`. The orderings may come out in any order. Assume the values are distinct. Use backtracking.
note: Track which indices are already used. Choose an unused value, explore the rest, then un-choose it before trying the next. There are n! orderings each of length n, so time is O(n * n!).
solution:
def permute(nums):
    res = []
    path = []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(path) == len(nums):
            res.append(path[:])
            return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            used[i] = True
            path.append(nums[i])
            backtrack()
            path.pop()
            used[i] = False
    backtrack()
    return res
cue: A `used` flag per index prevents repeats; record a copy of `path` once it reaches full length, and reset the flag when you pop.

prompt: Write `combine(n, k)` that returns every combination of `k` numbers chosen from 1 to n (inclusive). Order within a combination does not matter and the combinations may come out in any order. Use backtracking.
note: A `start` index keeps combinations moving forward so you never repeat a number or produce the same set twice. Choose a number, explore from the next index, then un-choose. Stop a branch as soon as `path` reaches length k.
cue: Pass a starting number so each choice only looks at larger numbers; record a copy once `path` has k of them.

prompt: Write `combination_sum(candidates, target)` that returns every unique combination of numbers from `candidates` that adds up to `target`. The same number may be used any number of times. Assume the candidates are distinct positive integers. Combinations may come out in any order. Use backtracking.
note: Recurse with the same index (not i + 1) to allow reusing a number, and never look backward so each combination is counted once. Track the remaining amount: succeed when it hits 0, abandon the branch when it goes negative.
cue: Subtract each chosen number from the remaining target; recurse from the same index so it can be picked again, and prune once the remaining amount drops below zero.

concept: pruning invalid branches with running counts
cue: Only add an open bracket while some remain, and only add a close bracket while unmatched opens exist; the first branch keeps opening first.
note: Backtracking gets faster when you cut off branches that cannot lead to a valid answer. Here the two count checks never let a close bracket appear without a matching open, so no invalid string is ever built.

prompt: Write `generate_parentheses(n)` that returns every string of `n` pairs of parentheses that is well formed (every open bracket is matched by a later close bracket). The strings may come out in any order. Use backtracking.
note: Choose the next character under two rules: add '(' only while opens used is below n, add ')' only while closes are behind opens. Explore by recursing on the longer string; the string is rebuilt each call so there is nothing to un-choose. Record a result when the length reaches 2n.
cue: Carry counts of opens and closes used so far; only open while below n, only close while closes trail opens, and save the string when it is full length.
syntax: if open_count < n: ... if close_count < open_count: ...

concept: subsets when the input has duplicates
prompt: Write `subsets_with_dup(nums)` that returns every unique subset of `nums`, which may contain repeated values. No two returned subsets should be equal, and they may come out in any order. Use backtracking.
note: Sort first so equal values sit together. Within one call, skip a value equal to the previous one you already tried at this level: that is the choose / explore / un-choose pattern plus a skip rule that stops duplicate subsets. Still up to O(n * 2^n).
solution:
def subsets_with_dup(nums):
    nums = sorted(nums)
    res = []
    path = []
    def backtrack(start):
        res.append(path[:])
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i - 1]:
                continue
            path.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            path.pop()
    backtrack(0)
    return res
cue: After sorting, inside each call skip any value equal to the one just to its left so a duplicate is not chosen twice at the same position.
syntax: if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i - 1]: continue
